questions.question: What is Peterson's solution? Explain its purpose in process synchronization.
questions.hint: Focus on its role in providing mutual exclusion and the variables used
keyPoints: Peterson's solution is a classic software-based synchronization algorithm
Used for solving the critical section problem in concurrent programming
Provides mutual exclusion for two or more processes accessing shared resources
Uses busy-waiting approach with flag and turn variables
Ensures that only one process can enter the critical section at a time

questions.question: Explain the two variables used in Peterson's solution and their roles.
questions.hint: Remember flag is an array and turn is a single variable
keyPoints: Variable 'flag' is a boolean array indicating whether a process wants to enter critical section
Variable 'turn' holds the process ID that has priority to enter critical section
flag[i] = true indicates process i wants to enter critical section
turn variable resolves conflict when both processes want to enter simultaneously
These variables are shared between processes for synchronization

questions.question: What is busy-waiting in the context of Peterson's solution? How is it handled?
questions.hint: Consider the while loop condition in the pseudocode
keyPoints: Busy-waiting is a technique where a process continuously checks a condition in a loop
In Peterson's solution, processes wait in a while loop until it's their turn
Also called spin-waiting as the process 'spins' while waiting
Wastes CPU cycles as the process is busy waiting without doing useful work
Modern operating systems prefer blocking over busy-waiting

questions.question: List and explain any three limitations of Peterson's solution.
questions.hint: Think about scalability and practical implementation issues
keyPoints: Limited to only two processes - cannot generalize to n processes easily
Uses busy-waiting which wastes CPU cycles
Assumes atomic operations on shared variables (flag and turn)
Prone to busy-wait in case of process failure in entry section
Not suitable for modern multi-core systems due to cache coherence issues

questions.question: Define the critical section problem and explain the three requirements that must be satisfied.
questions.hint: Remember the three fundamental requirements of critical section problem
keyPoints: Critical section is the portion of code that accesses shared resources
Mutual exclusion: Only one process should be in critical section at a time
Progress: Process not in critical section cannot block others from entering
Bounded waiting: There should be a limit on number of times other processes enter before a waiting process gets access
Peterson's solution satisfies all three requirements for two processes

questions.question: Compare Peterson's solution with semaphores as synchronization mechanisms.
keyPoints: Peterson's solution is a software-based algorithm; semaphores are OS-provided constructs
Peterson's uses busy-waiting; semaphores use blocking/waking mechanism
Peterson's limited to 2 processes; semaphores can handle n processes
Semaphores require hardware support (atomic operations); Peterson's assumes atomicity
Semaphores are more efficient as they don't waste CPU cycles in waiting

questions.question: Explain Peterson's solution algorithm in detail with proper pseudocode for two processes. Also explain the working of entry section, critical section, and exit section.
questions.hint: Write pseudocode for both Process 0 and Process 1
keyPoints: Define shared variables: boolean flag[2] and int turn
Initialize flag[0] = flag[1] = false
Entry section: flag[i] = true; turn = j; while(flag[j] && turn == j)
Critical section: The actual code accessing shared resource
Exit section: flag[i] = false indicating process exited critical section
Explain how mutual exclusion is achieved through flag and turn
Explain how bounded waiting is maintained through turn variable
Show that progress requirement is satisfied when process not interested

questions.question: Explain the reader-writer problem. How does Peterson's solution handle this problem?
questions.hint: Understand the difference between reader and writer synchronization requirements
keyPoints: Reader-writer problem involves multiple readers and writers accessing shared data
Multiple readers can read simultaneously but writers need exclusive access
Writer must not access resource when readers are reading
Peterson's solution uses flag and turn variables
flag indicates if writer is active/waiting
turn decides whose request (reader or writer) is served
Provides solution but with some limitations in throughput

questions.question: Prove that Peterson's solution satisfies the three requirements of critical section problem: mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting.
questions.hint: Use proof by contradiction for mutual exclusion
keyPoints: Mutual exclusion: Both flag[i] and flag[j] cannot be true simultaneously; turn ensures only one enters
Progress: If no process in critical section and one wants to enter, that process proceeds
Bounded waiting: When process j exits, it sets turn to i, giving i chance to enter
Show that process i will wait at most one turn (bounded by 1)
Use case analysis for different scenarios of processes wanting entry

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of Peterson's solution in modern operating systems.
questions.hint: Balance between theoretical importance and practical limitations
keyPoints: Advantages: Simple to understand, software-based, no special hardware needed
Advantages: Satisfies all three critical section requirements
Advantages: Works for two processes with proper implementation
Disadvantages: Busy-waiting consumes CPU cycles
Disadvantages: Limited to only two processes
Disadvantages: Assumes atomic memory accesses which may not hold on multi-core
Disadvantages: Not scalable; better alternatives like mutexes and semaphores exist

questions.question: Consider two processes P0 and P1 using Peterson's solution. Initially flag[0]=flag[1]=false and turn=0. Show step-by-step execution when P0 tries to enter critical section while P1 is already in critical section.
questions.hint: Trace the values of flag and turn variables at each step
keyPoints: Step 1: P0 sets flag[0]=true (wants to enter)
Step 2: P0 sets turn=1 (gives priority to P1)
Step 3: P0 checks while condition: flag[1] && turn==1
Step 4: Since flag[1]=true (P1 in critical section), condition is true
Step 5: P0 busy-waits in entry section
Step 6: When P1 exits, sets flag[1]=false
Step 7: P0's while condition becomes false, P0 enters critical section

questions.question: Two processes P0 and P1 attempt to enter critical section simultaneously using Peterson's algorithm. Trace the execution showing the values of flag[0], flag[1], and turn at each step until one enters.
questions.hint: Consider race condition in setting turn variable
keyPoints: Initial: flag[0]=false, flag[1]=false, turn=0
P0 executes: flag[0]=true, turn=1
P1 executes: flag[1]=true, turn=1 (turn already 1)
P0 checks: flag[1] && turn==1 is true (1 && 1), P0 waits
P1 checks: flag[0] && turn==1 is true (1 && 1), but turn=1 and P1 set it
This creates tie-breaker issue - depends on interleaving
OS scheduler determines which process proceeds first

questions.question: Design a solution using Peterson's algorithm for two processes P0 and P1 sharing a counter variable 'count'. Write the complete pseudocode showing how both processes safely increment the counter 1000 times.
questions.hint: Use the standard Peterson's algorithm structure
keyPoints: Declare shared variables: int count=0; boolean flag[2]={false,false}; int turn=0
For P0: do { flag[0]=true; turn=1; while(flag[1] && turn==1); count++; flag[0]=false; } while(true)
For P1: do { flag[1]=true; turn=0; while(flag[0] && turn==0); count++; flag[1]=false; } while(true)
Explain entry section ensures mutual exclusion
Explain critical section is count++ operation
Explain exit section resets flag
Note: Final count should be 2000 if both processes complete

questions.question: Explain what happens in Peterson's solution when one process fails in the entry section. How does this affect the other process?
questions.hint: Consider the scenario where flag remains true forever
keyPoints: If process fails in entry section (before busy-wait), other process may starve
If process sets flag to true but fails before entering critical section
Other process sees flag[failed]=true and may wait indefinitely
This is a fundamental problem with busy-wait solutions
Process failure in critical section can leave resource in inconsistent state
Modern solutions use semaphores with kernel intervention to handle such cases
Peterson's solution has no mechanism to detect or recover from process failure

questions.question: What assumptions are made in Peterson's solution regarding hardware and memory operations?
questions.hint: Think about memory models and hardware architecture
keyPoints: Assumes atomic read and write operations on flag and turn variables
Assumes memory writes by one process are immediately visible to others
Assumes no instruction reordering by compiler or CPU
Modern multi-core systems may violate these assumptions due to caching
Cache coherence issues can cause visibility problems
Memory barriers or fences may be needed to ensure visibility
